segments.text: All the data is open.

So if you want to know more about Lama, you can go bother Sean.

Another thing is DeepSeq, who made the DeepSeq codermodel,
is also created a very strong math model.
That's competitive with GPT-4 and a lot of math things.
Basically, the way they did this was they did this by training a classifier
to try to identify data on the web that is related to math
and scraping all of that data, in fact, doing that.
So you can get gold standard data from proof file
into a whole bunch of other sources.
And so they train data like math or not math classifier
in the harvest city of Mathe.

and actually ran out of time to do the math stuff.

But I don't think they're really doing a lot of things.
Like you could think of obvious things like doing RLHF
based on whether it gets the answer right or not.
And as far as I know, that's not a big ingredient here,
but I'll be more sure of that when we talk about it.
Cool.
And the final one, it's not a synth model.
It's a science model.
So I'm going to take one of them.
But this model Galactica was a model for science that was trained by Meta.
Does anyone remember this model?
Or was anybody around when this model came out?
No.
There was a big PR disaster for Meta when they released this model.
Because they said, this is a great model for math.
Use it in writing your science paper.
Oh, sorry.
This is a great model for science.
Try using it in your science papers.
And this came out about two years ago.
And two years ago, language models hallucinated all the time
and came up with false scientific facts and stuff.
And so basically, a lot of people kind of bashed this model.
In my mind, kind of unfairly, because they actually
have a lot of really interesting things in this paper.
One interesting thing in this paper
is they tried to create a general purpose model for science
that's able to understand not only text,
but also various modalities of scientific data.
And so that includes text.
It includes latex equations.
It includes code.
But it also included things like molecular structures
and like, collagen and DNA and stuff like this.
So they tried to model biology and other things like this as well.
So I think it's really kind of too bad
that this model got a bad rap because I really like the work
that went into it.
And I hope we'll see more of this.
Because language models for scientists
are really big topics that a lot of people
are thinking about.
Cool.
One thing I didn't talk about is multimodal models.
But I hope to talk about multimodal models in a future class.
So I'll talk more about that.
See, the next thing is closed models.
So closed models, we don't know a whole lot about them.
Most of what we know about them in their training data
and other things like that is their is conjecture.
So the standard format for releasing a closed model,
or not releasing, but publicizing a closed model,
is people will write a blog post and they'll write a paper.
And generally what the paper does
is it only talks about evaluation.
It only talks about how good the model is on various things,
how safe it is, how they put a lot of effort into red
keaming the model so that it doesn't do bad things
and stuff like that.
And it tells you nothing about how they actually built the model.
So mostly what I can talk about are capabilities
is opposed to talk about our capabilities
is opposed to what actually went into the model.
So there's GPT-4.
GPT-4, I think everybody knows, it's kind of the de facto
standard strong language model.
It used to be the only strong language model.
It used to be on its own the strongest language model,
and there were no real competitors to GPT-4
from that point of view.
I think still, if I wanted a strong language model
for just something that I'm going to do randomly,
I still rely on GPT-4 more than anything else
to give me a really good answer.
But there are now other competitors, like the five-fold.
So GPT-4 anyway, it powers the pro version of chat GPT.
It was tuned to be good as a chat-based assistant.
It accepts image inputs and it supports calling external tools
through a function calling interface.
I think people are generally familiar with this,
but just in case you're not, I'd like to show a few things
that I like to do.
So let me...
So I'll just randomly grab one of my papers from archives.
My most recent paper.
And...
I can copy paste this...
And write a kind of this into JSON format.
And they drop it in here.
And...
So this is an exhibit of its multi-modal abilities
because I can throw in a table.
And it basically turns it into JSON format.
So I actually turned a fair amount of data
that I created and creating these slides into JSON format
so I can save it later for whatever I want it for.
And I did it through this.
This is an example of the multi-modal abilities.
I can also tell you about images and stuff like that.
So also, there was a famous article written by Gary Marcus
that said deep learning is hitting a wall.
It basically was written two years ago.
And Gary Marcus was saying deep learning doesn't...
It's not the way for the future.
We're going to need things other than deep learning
in order to be able to make progress.
And whether you believe that is true or not,
I will let you to your own opinion.
But I could also say create a picture of deep learning
breaking through a brick wall
and it can generate images for you.
Of course, if you ever do a live demo,
even if it's a live demo of open AI's product
that a million people use, it will break when you try to do it.
So this is another thing that I can do.
So there we have a picture of deep learning breaking through a brick wall
and it can generate images and stuff.
So these are the kinds of things that I now expect.
So it's not just like reasoning ability and other stuff like that.
It's also multi-modality being able to generate code.
Another thing that's kind of nice is make a histogram of these numbers.
One, two.
One, two, four.
So it can do code generation and display the results for you.
There are efforts to make open source language models
be able to do these things.
And in order to do this, you need multi-modality.
You need also the ability to use tools.
